claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. 

To start, I need to understand a bit about your child (or children):

**How old is your child?** (Or if you have multiple children you're asking about, please tell me their ages)

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Young people **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please share your child's age, and if you have more than one child, let me know all of their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have three children actually - Alex is 10, Blake is 19, and Charlie is 2.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children:
- **Alex** (age 10)
- **Blake** (age 19)
- **Charlie** (age 2)

Let me assess each child's eligibility. I'll start with the youngest and work through each one.

## For Charlie (age 2):

Charlie is under 16, so let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 2):** Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

I assume at age 2, Charlie is not working, correct? Let me continue with that assumption and we'll move to Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie:** Is Charlie receiving any benefits in their own right (like Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

Again, I'll assume no for a 2-year-old and continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie:** Is Charlie in any apprenticeship program?

I'll assume no for a 2-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, I need to check your residency status (this applies to you as the claimant for all three children):

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

Or do any of these special circumstances apply to you:
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have pre-settled status and are working, studying, or actively seeking work
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Do you have responsibility for your children?** This means:
- You live with them, OR
- You contribute to their upkeep (food, clothes, housing, etc.)

I assume all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) live with you in Manchester - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex and Blake live with me, but Charlie doesn't live with me - they live with their mum.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So:
- **Alex (10)** and **Blake (19)** live with you
- **Charlie (2)** lives with their mum

For Alex and Blake who live with you, you clearly have responsibility. Let me continue with them.

For Charlie, an important question: **Do you contribute to Charlie's upkeep?** This could include:
- Paying maintenance (weekly, monthly, or lump sum)
- Contributing money for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, presents
- The amount should be at least equivalent to what Child Benefit would pay

Do you contribute financially to Charlie's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially to Charlie's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. That means:
- **Alex (10)** and **Blake (19)**: You have responsibility (they live with you) ✓
- **Charlie (2)**: You don't have responsibility (doesn't live with you and you're not contributing to their upkeep)

**For Charlie**, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit since you don't meet the responsibility requirement. Charlie's mum would be the one who could claim Child Benefit for Charlie.

Let me continue the assessment for **Alex and Blake**. Let me proceed with the next check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Alex and Blake:** Is anyone else (such as another parent or guardian) currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Let me continue with the next check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex and Blake:** Are either of them currently:
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Alex and Blake:** Is the local council paying towards either child's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to go back and check Blake's situation more carefully since Blake is 19 years old.

Let me restart Blake's assessment from the beginning to make sure we cover the education/training requirements properly:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 19):** This is important - since Blake is 19, they need to be in approved education or training to qualify. 

**Is Blake currently:**
1. In education or training (like college, sixth form, A-levels, or approved training)?
2. Has Blake recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)?
3. Neither of the above?

What's Blake's current situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't in education or training anymore - they left school and they're not doing any courses or anything like that now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. One more question about Blake:

**When did Blake leave education?** Specifically, was it within the last 20 weeks (roughly 5 months)?

This matters because there's a short extension period after leaving education where you might still be eligible for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, it's been longer than that - Blake left school a while ago now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me now provide you with the complete eligibility assessment for all three children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results**

Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your three children:

---

### ✅ **Alex (age 10) - ELIGIBLE**

**You can claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Alex meets all the qualifying criteria:
- Under 16 years old
- Lives with you in Manchester
- Not working full-time
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- Local council not paying for their care
- You meet UK residency requirements

---

### ❌ **Blake (age 19) - NOT ELIGIBLE**

**You cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake.**

Blake doesn't qualify because:
- At 19 years old, they fall into the 16-19 age category where they **must be in approved education or training** to qualify
- Blake is not currently in education or training
- Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago, so the extension period no longer applies

**Note:** If Blake returns to approved education or training (such as college, A-levels, or approved vocational training), you could become eligible to claim for them again.

---

### ❌ **Charlie (age 2) - NOT ELIGIBLE (for you)**

**You cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie.**

While Charlie is the right age (under 16), you don't meet the **responsibility requirement** because:
- Charlie doesn't live with you
- You don't contribute financially to Charlie's upkeep

**Important:** Charlie's mother, who Charlie lives with, would be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie (assuming she meets all other requirements).

---

## **Next Steps**

**For Alex:**
1. You can apply for Child Benefit online or by post
2. Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim
3. You'll need Alex's birth certificate and your National Insurance number

**For Blake:**
If Blake enrolls in approved education or training in the future, you can then apply or reinstate the claim.

**For Charlie:**
Charlie's mother should apply for Child Benefit if she hasn't already.

Do you have any questions about these results or need help with the application process for Alex?

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, is not working full-time, is not receiving benefits in their own right, is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, and the local council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. You meet all residency requirements as you normally live in the UK. Therefore, Alex qualifies as an eligible child for Child Benefit.

Blake is 19 years old, which means they fall into the 16-19 age category where they must be in approved education or training to qualify for Child Benefit. Blake is not currently in education or training, and left education more than 20 weeks ago, so they do not qualify for the extension period. Therefore, Blake does not qualify as an eligible child for Child Benefit.

Charlie is 2 years old (under 16), which would normally make them a qualifying child. However, Charlie does not live with you and you do not contribute financially to their upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, you must either live with the child OR contribute to their maintenance to be considered responsible for them. Since you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for them. Charlie's mother, who Charlie lives with, would be the appropriate person to claim.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have three children with different eligibility outcomes. Alex (10) fully qualifies and you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for them. Blake (19) does not qualify because they are not in approved education or training. Charlie (2) would be a qualifying child, but you cannot claim for them because you don't have responsibility (Charlie doesn't live with you and you don't contribute to their upkeep).

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible
